summary: This notebook provides a production-ready inference pipeline for the RSNA Intracranial Aneurysm Detection competition, demonstrating how to robustly process 3D DICOM series and generate multi-label aneurysm location predictions. It shows how to convert volumetric medical scans into compact 3-channel inputs (middle slice, MIP, and standard deviation projection) while applying modality-specific windowing and metadata integration. The code highlights practical deployment patterns for Kaggle, including Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) to stabilize outputs, weighted model ensembling, and strict memory/directory cleanup to prevent server crashes. Readers should take away a reliable template for handling DICOM metadata, projecting 3D volumes into 2D channels, and structuring inference servers that gracefully handle edge cases and fallback predictions.
competition_flow: Reads DICOM series from a directory, extracts and processes slices into 3-channel images with patient metadata, passes them through pre-trained CNN/Transformer models with TTA, and outputs a submission DataFrame with aneurysm location probabilities.
data_reading: Uses pydicom.dcmread to parse .dcm files from a series directory, extracting pixel arrays and DICOM tags (Modality, PatientAge, PatientSex, RescaleSlope/Intercept).
Leverages polars and pandas for DataFrame construction and kaggle_evaluation.rsna_inference_server for competition API integration.
data_processing: Applies modality-specific DICOM windowing (CT, CTA, MRA, MRI) and rescales pixel values using RescaleSlope/Intercept.

Resizes each slice to 512x512, samples or pads exactly 32 slices per series, and constructs a 3-channel input from the middle slice, maximum intensity projection (MIP), and standard deviation projection.
Normalizes channels to [0, 255] and applies ImageNet normalization via Albumentations during inference.
Implements Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) with horizontal flip, vertical flip, and 90-degree rotation, averaging predictions across 4 transforms.
features_engineering: Extracts patient age and sex from DICOM headers, normalizes age to [0, 1], and feeds them as a 2D metadata tensor through a small 2-layer MLP before concatenating with image features.
ensembling: Combines predictions from EfficientNetV2-S, ConvNeXt-Small, and Swin-Small using weighted averaging (0.4, 0.3, 0.3 respectively) when the ensemble mode is enabled.
insights: Modality-specific DICOM windowing parameters are critical for preserving diagnostic contrast across different scan types.
Stacking a middle slice, MIP, and standard deviation projection creates a highly informative 3-channel representation that captures both anatomical detail and volumetric distribution.
Integrating lightweight patient metadata via a separate MLP branch improves model calibration without adding significant computational overhead.
TTA with geometric flips and rotations effectively reduces prediction variance and stabilizes outputs during inference.
